Isotopic fractionation is defined as the relative partitioning of the heavier and lighter isotopes between two coexisting phases in a natural system.

## Contents

# Isotope fractionation

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **is missing information** about **other fields where isotopic fractionation is observed, e.g., metabolism of natural products, and all methods of analysis thereof, including MS and NMR, all being given balanced treatment**. Please expand the article to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Isotope_fractionation "Talk:Isotope fractionation"). *(March 2025)* |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/6c/Wiki_letter_w.svg/44px-Wiki_letter_w.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Thermal_ionization_mass_spectrometer.jpg/250px-Thermal_ionization_mass_spectrometer.jpg)

**Isotope fractionation** describes [fractionation](/wiki/Fractionation "Fractionation") processes that affect the relative abundance of isotopes, a phenomena that occurs (and so advantage is taken of it) in the study [geochemistry](/wiki/Isotope_geochemistry "Isotope geochemistry"),[[1]](#cite_note-USGS-1) biochemistry,[[2]](#cite_note-PNMRS2020-2) food science,[[3]](#cite_note-ABC2003-3) and other fields. Normally, the focus is on [stable isotopes](/wiki/Stable_isotope "Stable isotope") of the same element. Isotopic fractionation can be measured by [isotope analysis](/wiki/Isotope_analysis "Isotope analysis"), using [isotope-ratio mass spectrometry](/wiki/Isotope-ratio_mass_spectrometry "Isotope-ratio mass spectrometry"),[[1]](#cite_note-USGS-1) [nuclear magnetic resonance](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Nuclear magnetic resonance") methods ([specialised techniques](/wiki/Isotopic_analysis_by_nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Isotopic analysis by nuclear magnetic resonance"),[[2]](#cite_note-PNMRS2020-2)[[3]](#cite_note-ABC2003-3)) [cavity ring-down spectroscopy](/wiki/Cavity_ring-down_spectroscopy "Cavity ring-down spectroscopy"),[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] etc., to measure [ratios of isotopes](/wiki/Stable_isotope_ratio "Stable isotope ratio"), important tools to understand geochemical and biological systems, past and present.[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For example, [biochemical](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry") processes cause changes in ratios of stable [carbon isotopes](/wiki/Isotopes_of_carbon "Isotopes of carbon") incorporated into biomass.[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Definition

Stable isotopes partitioning between two substances *A* and *B* can be expressed by the use of the isotopic fractionation factor (alpha):

where *R* is the ratio of the heavy to light isotope (e.g., 2H/1H or 18O/16O). Values for alpha tend to be very close to 1.[[1]](#cite_note-USGS-1)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Types

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion** with: a source-derived verification and explanation of the current appearing technique names, which is generally, alone, just jargon to even informed readers. You can help by [adding to it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Isotope_fractionation&action=edit&section=). *(March 2025)* |

![[icon]](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg/44px-Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg.png)

There are four types of isotope fractionation (of which the first two are normally most important): [equilibrium fractionation](/wiki/Equilibrium_fractionation "Equilibrium fractionation"), [kinetic fractionation](/wiki/Kinetic_fractionation "Kinetic fractionation"), [mass-independent fractionation](/wiki/Mass-independent_fractionation "Mass-independent fractionation") (or non-mass-dependent fractionation), and [transient kinetic isotope fractionation](/wiki/Transient_kinetic_isotope_fractionation "Transient kinetic isotope fractionation").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Example

Isotope fractionation occurs during a [phase transition](/wiki/Phase_transition "Phase transition"), when the ratio of light to heavy isotopes in the involved molecules changes. As Carol Kendall of the [USGS](/wiki/USGS "USGS") states in an information page for the USGS Isotope Tracers Project, "[water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor") condenses (an [equilibrium process](/wiki/Equilibrium_fractionation "Equilibrium fractionation")), the heavier water isotopes (18O and 2H) become enriched in the liquid phase while the lighter isotopes (16O and 1H) tend toward the vapor phase".[[1]](#cite_note-USGS-1)

FULL CONTENT:
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)

# isotopic fractionation

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

**isotopic fractionation**, enrichment of one [isotope](https://www.britannica.com/science/isotope) relative to another in a chemical or physical process. Two isotopes of an element are different in weight but not in gross chemical properties, which are determined by the number of electrons. However, subtle chemical effects do result from the difference in mass of isotopes. Isotopes of an element may have slightly different [equilibrium](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/equilibrium) constants for a particular [chemical reaction](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemical-reaction), so that slightly different amounts of reaction products are made from reactants containing different isotopes. This leads to isotopic fractionation, the extent of which can be expressed by a [fractionation factor](https://www.britannica.com/science/fractionation-factor), alpha (α), also known as a separation factor, or enrichment factor. This factor is the ratio of the concentrations of the two isotopes in one [compound](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/compound) divided by the ratio in the other compound. If *N**l* and *N**h* stand for the relative abundances of the light and heavy isotopes, respectively, in the original compound and if *n**l* and *n**h* are the corresponding abundances in the new compound, then α = (*N**l*/*N**h*)/(*n**l*/*n**h*). The fractionation factor is the factor by which the abundance ratio of two isotopes will change during a chemical reaction or a physical process.

The precipitation of [calcium carbonate](https://www.britannica.com/science/calcium-carbonate) from water is an example of an equilibrium fractionation process. During this precipitation [oxygen-18](https://www.britannica.com/science/oxygen-18) is enriched by a factor of 2.5 percent relative to the lighter, more common isotope oxygen-16; the fractionation factor depends on the temperature and, consequently, can be used as a means of determining the temperature of the water in which the precipitation occurs. This is the basis of the so-called oxygen isotope geothermometer.

During the process of [photosynthesis](https://www.britannica.com/science/photosynthesis), carbon-12, the most common isotope of [carbon](https://www.britannica.com/science/carbon-chemical-element), is enriched further relative to the heavier isotope, carbon-13; the cellulose and lignin in wood from trees is enriched by a factor of about 2.5 percent during this process. The fractionation in this case is not an equilibrium process but rather a kinetic effect: the lighter isotope proceeds faster through the photosynthetic process and, consequently, is enriched.

![Dow Chemical Company](https://cdn.britannica.com/14/190914-050-305BD63E/Dow-Chemical-plant-South-Charleston-West-Virginia.jpg)

Physical processes, such as evaporation and condensation and thermal [diffusion](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/diffusion), may also result in significant fractionation. For example, [oxygen-16](https://www.britannica.com/science/oxygen-16) is enriched relative to the heavier oxygen isotopes in water evaporating from the sea. On the other hand, any precipitate is enriched in the heavy isotope, resulting in a further concentration of oxygen-16 in atmospheric water vapour. Because the processes of evaporation and condensation tend to occur in the equatorial regions and [polar regions](https://www.britannica.com/science/polar-region), respectively, snow in the polar regions is depleted in oxygen-18 by about 5 percent now compared to the surrounding ocean. As the ratio of oxygen isotopes in precipitates is sensitive to small changes in temperature at the time of [deposition](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/deposition), measurements of polar ice cores are useful in studying [climate change](https://www.britannica.com/science/climate-change).

The fissile isotope [uranium-235](https://www.britannica.com/science/uranium-235) has been separated from the more abundant, nonfissile isotope uranium-238 by exploiting the slight difference in the rates at which the gaseous hexafluorides of the two isotopes pass through a porous barrier.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Isotopic fractionation is the preferential partitioning of isotopes between phases, crucial for understanding geochemical processes and interpreting isotopic data across various scientific fields.
This process occurs due to differences in mass, leading to variations in reaction rates, where lighter isotopes often react faster, affecting the products formed.
Understanding fractionation effects is essential for accurate interpretation of isotopic data, influencing conclusions in climate studies, biochemistry, and environmental science.
